column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
The intake milestone involves setting up the rules and game plan before starting a big team project. The team figures out how they will work together, who does what, and what the main goals are. They also plan out the steps they need to take, like making a schedule and deciding on priorities. It's like getting everyone on the same page with a clear map of where they're going. The team creates tools like a Kanban board to track progress, a statement of work to outline tasks, and charts to show who is responsible for what.

The value of this milestone is that it helps the team work smoothly and efficiently. By having clear roles and a shared understanding of goals, everyone knows what to do and when. This organization helps the team stay focused and make sure they are moving in the right direction. For the product, it means that the team can deliver what they promised on time and meet the needs of those who will use it.
column7: The "Intake" milestone is like the very first step in planning a big party. Imagine you want to throw a party, but before you can decide on the games, food, or decorations, you need to figure out the basics, like what kind of party it will be and who will do what. This milestone is all about setting up the team, deciding everyone's roles, and making sure everyone agrees on how to work together. It's crucial to complete this step on time because it sets the foundation for everything else. Without it, the rest of the planning can't happen smoothly, just like you can't have a great party without knowing the basics first!
column8:
The milestone is the first in order. It should be done before all product milestones.

Think of the product lifecycle like planning a big party. The "Intake" milestone is like deciding you want to have a party and figuring out the basic idea of what kind of party it will be. Before you can start planning the details, like what games to play or what food to serve, you need to know that you want to have a party in the first place. So, the "Intake" is the very first step, and nothing comes before it.

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
The Continuous Discovery milestone is like a detective mission where the team gathers clues and insights to understand what people want and need. They do this by talking to users, testing ideas, and studying the market. Tasks include creating surveys, testing prototypes, and observing how people use products. The team then organizes their findings into reports and plans, like a Go-To-Market Plan or a Business Plan, to guide the project forward.

The value of this milestone is that it helps teams make informed decisions by understanding their audience better. It ensures that the product is designed to meet real needs, making it more likely to succeed. For teams, it means having a clear direction and strategy, while for products, it means being more user-friendly and competitive in the market.
column7: Imagine you're planning a big party. First, you decide what kind of party you want (Initial Discovery). Then, you keep talking to your friends to see what they like and what would make the party awesome (Continuous Discovery). This step is super important because it helps you gather all the cool ideas and make sure everyone will have a great time. You try out different games and snacks to see which ones are the most fun (like A/B Prototype Testing) and ask your friends for their opinions (User Acceptance Testing). By doing this, you can plan the best party ever, making sure everything is just right before you start setting up (Design and Development). Completing this step on time means your party will be a hit, and everyone will be excited to come!
column8:
The milestone is the third in order. It should be done before Design and Development, and should be done after Initial Discovery. 

Imagine you're planning a big party. First, you decide what kind of party you want (Initial Discovery). Then, you keep talking to your friends to see what they like and what would make the party awesome (Continuous Discovery). After that, you start getting everything ready, like buying snacks and setting up decorations (MVP Development).

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
During the MMP Scope milestone, the team figures out the best path to take and what they need to bring along. They start by deciding on the overall strategy, gathering all the necessary information, and officially kicking off the project. They write down what needs to be done, refine these ideas, and estimate how much effort each task will take. They also calculate how fast they can work, keep the project plan updated, and define the vision for the final release. The team delivers a detailed plan of what will be included in the release, a list of features, a refined list of tasks, and a timeline chart.

The value of this milestone is that it helps the team stay organized and focused, ensuring everyone knows what to do and when. For the product, it means having a clear plan that guides its development, making sure it meets the goals and is delivered on time.
column7: The MVP Scope milestone is like planning the essentials for a big party. It's all about figuring out the most important things we need to make our product launch successful. This includes deciding on the main features and creating a clear plan, just like choosing a venue and making a guest list for a party. Completing this milestone on time is crucial because it sets the foundation for everything that comes next, like designing and developing the product. If we don't finish this step on schedule, it could delay the whole launch, just like if you don't book a venue in time, your party might not happen as planned.
column8:
The milestone is the fourth in order. It should be done before MVP Design Requirements and MVP Development Requirements. It should be done after Initial Discovery. 

Imagine you're planning a big party. First, you decide what kind of party you want (Initial Discovery). Then, you figure out the most important things you need to make it happen, like a venue and a guest list (MVP Scope). After that, you start planning the details, like decorations and food (MVP Design Requirements and MVP Development Requirements).

column6:
The MMP Vision milestone is where you build a long-term definition of the outcomes you want to achieve and problems to solve as a team. 

This milestone is like planning a big adventure where the team figures out the best path to take and what they need to succeed. They work on tasks like deciding what the project will look like, what makes it special, and how to tell others about it. They also gather information about what customers want and how to reach them. The team creates helpful tools like vision boards, pitch decks, and roadmaps to guide their journey.

The value of this milestone is that it helps the team stay organized and focused, making sure everyone knows the plan and their role in it. For the product, it ensures that it meets customer needs and stands out in the market, increasing its chances of success.
column7: The MMP Vision milestone is like setting the stage for a big school play. Before the show can go on, you need to know what the play is about, who the characters are, and how everything will come together. This milestone helps everyone understand the big picture of the product launch by defining what the product will be, who it’s for, and how it will stand out. Completing it on time is crucial because it guides all the next steps, like designing and building the product. If this part isn't done right, it’s like trying to perform a play without a script or plan, which could lead to confusion and mistakes later on.
column8: The milestone is the third in order. It should be done before MMP Design Requirements and MMP Development Requirements. It should be done after Initial Discovery.

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
The Systems Architecture milestone is like building the blueprint for a new city. The team works on designing the technical plans, setting up security rules, checking for safety, and creating maps that show how everything connects. They also build the foundation for storing information and create pathways for data to travel. The tasks include making technical plans, setting up security checks, and creating systems for storing and sharing information. The deliverables are like the city's infrastructure: hosting platforms, database designs, detailed maps of the system, and tools for connecting different parts of the system.

The value of this milestone is that it provides a strong and secure foundation for the project. For teams, it means having clear plans and guidelines to follow, which makes building the rest of the project easier and more organized. For products, it ensures that everything is set up safely and efficiently, so the final product works smoothly and securely.
column7: The Systems Architecture milestone is like building the blueprint for a new house before construction starts. It's all about planning how everything will fit together, from the rooms to the wiring. In the product launch process, this milestone ensures that all the technical parts, like databases and security systems, are set up correctly. Completing it on time is crucial because it lays the foundation for the next steps, like developing the actual product features. If this planning stage is delayed, it can hold up the entire project, just like how a house can't be built without a solid plan.
column8: The milestone is the fifth in order. It should be done before MVP Development Requirements and should be done after MVP Development Requirements.

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
During the MVP Design Requirements milestone, the team figures out all details they need to design a new product in the MVP. They gather information, decide what features are most important, and write down all the details. It's like creating a map for a treasure hunt, where they list tasks like interviewing people, writing stories about how users will interact with the product, and designing how everything will look and feel. They also create guides like a sitemap, user journey maps, and brand identity to make sure everyone knows the plan.

The value of this milestone is huge for teams and products because it sets a clear path for everyone to follow. It helps the team understand what they need to build and why, making sure they don't miss anything important. For the product, it ensures that it meets the needs of the users and stands out with a strong brand identity. This organized approach saves time and effort, leading to a successful and well-received product.
column7: Imagine you're planning a big school play. First, you have a vision of what the play will be about (MVP Vision). Then, you decide what parts of the play are most important to include (MVP Scope). Once you know what the play is about and what's important, you figure out what the costumes and sets should look like (MVP Design Requirements). This step is crucial because it sets the stage for everything that comes next. If you don't know what the costumes and sets should look like, you can't start making them. Completing this milestone on time ensures that everyone knows exactly what to do and when, so the play can go on without a hitch.
column8:
The milestone is the fifth in order. It should be done before the MVP Design milestone and should be done after the MVP Vision and MVP Scope milestones. 

Imagine you're planning a big school play. First, you have a vision of what the play will be about (MVP Vision). Then, you decide what parts of the play are most important to include (MVP Scope). Once you know what the play is about and what's important, you figure out what the costumes and sets should look like (MVP Design Requirements). After that, you can start actually designing and creating those costumes and sets (MVP Design).

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
The MVP Development milestone is like building the first version of a new video game. The team works on creating the game's look and feel (Front-End Coding), making sure the game runs smoothly behind the scenes (Server-Side Coding), and setting up the rules and connections (Creating REST APIs, Technical Architecture Creation, Entity Relationship Creation). They also write down how everything works (Documenting Code) and make sure the game can be played on different devices (Deploying Code). Finally, they add special features like secure trading (Blockchain Coding). The end result is a playable version of the game, complete with instructions and special features (Code, Coding Documentation, API Library, Smart Contracts).

The value of this milestone is huge for teams and products. For teams, it means they have a working version of their project that they can test and improve. It helps them see what works and what needs fixing. For products, it means they have something real to show to users and get feedback on, which is super important for making the final version even better.
column7: Imagine you're building a treehouse, and you've just finished planning what you want it to look like. Now, it's time to actually build the basic structure, which is like the MVP Development milestone. This step is super important because it's where you put together all the essential parts, like the walls and floor, using tasks like coding and creating APIs. If you don't finish this on time, you can't move on to adding cool features or decorations later. Completing this milestone means you have a solid foundation, just like having a sturdy treehouse to play in, and it ensures everything works smoothly when you launch your product.
column8:
The milestone is the sixth in order. It should be done after MVP Development Requirements and should be done before nothing. 

Imagine you're building a treehouse. First, you need to plan what you want in your treehouse (that's like the MVP Development Requirements). Once you know what you need, you start building the basic structure of the treehouse (that's the MVP Development). After this, you don't have any specific steps to follow immediately, but you might want to think about adding more features or decorations later.

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
During the MVP Design milestone, the team creates designs and tests features for MVP to see if they work well. They make things like logos, icons, and illustrations, and put together a library of design components. They also create wireframes and prototypes, which are like rough drafts and polished versions of the app. The team writes the text for the app, organizes the information, and makes sure everything is easy to find and use. They also test how accessible the app is and how well it can be found on search engines.

The value of this milestone is that it helps the team understand what the app or website will look like and how it will work. It ensures that the design is consistent and appealing, and that users can easily navigate and use the product. This step is crucial for making sure the final product meets the needs of its users and stands out in the market.
column7: The MVP Design milestone is like creating a blueprint for a new invention. It's super important because it helps us figure out how the product will look and feel before it's actually built. Imagine trying to build a treehouse without a plan—you might end up with something wobbly or not very fun to use! By completing this milestone on time, we make sure everything from the colors and logos to the way users interact with the product is just right. This step includes designing components, testing with users, and making sure the product is easy to use and accessible. It's like making sure all the pieces of a puzzle fit perfectly together, so when the product is launched, it's ready to impress and work smoothly for everyone.
column8: The milestone is the sixth in order. It should be done before the MVP Design Requirements milestone and should be done after the MVP Design Requirements milestone.

column6:
This milestone is like a big check-up for a project to make sure everything works well and looks good. The team does things like testing the project to find any bugs, creating automatic tests to save time, writing down what the project needs to do, checking how easy it is for everyone to use, making sure it shows up well in search engines, and analyzing how user-friendly it is. They deliver a plan for testing, detailed test cases, an analysis of how the project is doing, scenarios that show how it should behave, and a report on how accessible it is.

The value of this milestone is that it helps the team catch problems early, making the project better and more reliable. For the product, it means it will be easier to use, more visible online, and accessible to everyone, which can lead to happier users and more success.
column7: The Quality Assurance milestone is like a big check-up for our product before it goes out into the world. Imagine if you were baking a cake for a party; you'd want to make sure it tastes great and looks amazing before serving it to your friends. That's what this milestone does for our product. We test it to make sure everything works perfectly, create plans to catch any mistakes, and ensure it's easy for everyone to use, including people with disabilities. By completing this milestone on time, we make sure our product is ready to impress and succeed when it finally launches.
column8: The milestone is the sixth in order. It should be done before MVP Development and should be done after MVP Development.

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
The MVP Alpha Testing milestone is like a trial run for a new app or website, where the team checks if everything works well and is easy to use. They do this by testing how users interact with it, making sure it's accessible to everyone, and seeing how it shows up in search engines. They also look at how easy it is to navigate and gather feedback from users. The team collects all this information to make sure the app or website is ready for more people to use. The tasks include testing how users accept the product, researching user experience, checking accessibility, designing for search engines, analyzing usability, and gathering feedback through surveys and research.

The value of this milestone is that it helps the team understand what works and what doesn't before the product is fully launched. It ensures that the product is user-friendly, accessible, and meets the needs of its users. For the team, it provides important feedback and insights that guide improvements, making the final product better and more successful.
column7: The MVP Alpha Testing milestone is like a big check-up for our product before it goes out into the world. Imagine if you were getting ready for a big school play; you'd want to practice and make sure everything is perfect, right? That's what we're doing here. We're testing how easy it is to use, making sure everyone can access it, and seeing if it works well with search engines. We gather feedback from people who try it out, just like asking friends to watch your rehearsal and tell you what they think. Completing this on time is super important because it helps us catch any problems early and make improvements, ensuring our product is ready to shine when it's launched!
column8: The milestone is the seventh in order. It should be done before Quality Assurance, MVP Development, and MVP Design. It should be done after Quality Assurance and MVP Development.

column4: A measured outcome of work progress.
column6:
The MVP Launch milestone is like getting ready for a big school play. First, the team makes sure everything works by testing it a lot, like practicing lines and scenes. They create a plan for testing, write down what needs to be done, and make sure the product is easy to use and find online. They also gather feedback from people who will use it, like asking friends if they like the play. Finally, they put together a plan for the big release, like setting the date for the play and making sure everyone knows their part.

The value of this milestone is huge for teams and products. For teams, it helps everyone stay on the same page and ensures that the product is ready for the world, just like a well-rehearsed play. For the product, it means it's polished, user-friendly, and meets the needs of the people who will use it, ensuring a successful launch and happy users.
column7: The MVP Launch milestone is like the big reveal of a new video game you've been working on with your friends. It's super important because it's when you show everyone what you've created and see if it works well. Before this, you need to make sure everything is tested and ready, like checking if the game levels are fun and if the controls work smoothly. You also gather feedback from people who try it out to make sure they enjoy it. Completing this milestone on time is crucial because it sets the stage for the final product launch, ensuring everything is polished and ready for the world to see. If you miss this step, it could delay the whole project, just like if you forgot to finish a big school project before the due date!
column8: The milestone is the ninth in order. It should be done before nothing and should be done after MVP Alpha Testing, MVP Beta Testing, and Quality Assurance.
